summary: U-Boot starts and loads kernel, ramdisk, and device tree.
evidence: The log shows the execution of U-Boot commands to load the kernel, ramdisk, and device tree. It also mentions the loading of environment from SPI Flash and the verification of hash integrity for each subimage.

summary: U-Boot initializes memory and sets up the system.
evidence: The log details the initialization of memory, DMA memory pools, and CMA memory pool. It also mentions the creation of a CMA memory pool at 0x9d000000, size 32 MiB, and a DMA memory pool at 0x99000000, size 64 MiB.

summary: U-Boot starts the Linux kernel.
evidence: The log shows the execution of commands to start the Linux kernel. It mentions the loading of the kernel image, ramdisk, and device tree.

summary: Software initialization and core configuration.
evidence: The log shows the execution of the 'montana' command with specific parameters, including a TDF file and an IPC configuration file. It also details the process of connecting to the IPC API, initializing it, and discovering cores using IPC.

summary: Reconfiguration and core setup.
evidence: The log records events related to reconfiguration, including the detection of IO_V1_CLTAP A0 on JTAG chain 0, warnings about missing TAP devices, and the use of XDPA as the OpenIPC connection. It also shows the process of setting up the SOC Core logical view, enumerating cores based on logical order, and Renumbering core taps.

summary: Hardware and software patching.
evidence: The log indicates the detection of PCUDATA component patching for io0 die, io1 die, and compute0 die. It also shows attempts to automatically load these patches using timestamp reading. This suggests a process of hardware and software patching.

summary: Core group manipulation.
evidence: The log shows the 'GPC core group' halts and resumes on specific devices. This suggests a process of manipulating core groups for testing or debugging purposes.

summary: CSPs (Google, AWS, Oracle) planning to deploy PCH Ignition FW. ZBB feature will impact MCTP support for them.
evidence:
OOBMSM as MCTP bus owner – ZBB feature discussion
... CSPs are identified as potential CSPs which are planning to deploy PCH Ignition FW.

summary: MCTP is the transport layer enabling application protocols like PLDM and VDM for Node manager, Virtual RAID VROC OOB Mgmt, SDSi, Platform monitoring telemetry.
evidence:
MCTP is the transport layer which allows application layer protocols like PLDM, vendor defined messages -VDM to abstract out physical layer functionalities.
... These protocols in turn are used for enabling Node manager, Virtual RAID VROC OOB Mgmt – Enterprise (HP, Lenovo), SDSi, Platform monitoring telemetry -PMT, Persistent memory management etc.

summary: Google's plan to enable MCTP in DVT phase with CrowPass (CPS) DIMMs. Google faces challenges due to lack of OOB monitoring capabilities.
evidence:
Google feedback
... Plan to start enabling MCTP in DVT phase with CrowPass (CPS) DIMMs. Use case around Out of Band Management over PCIe.

summary: Google's OOB monitoring requirements and limitations.
evidence:
Google feedback
... Google has hard time requirement of <1 min polling on OOB health and performance monitoring data (status, temp., % lifetime, bandwidth, etc.). 
... Polling modules on fully populated 2S system takes 22 sec. over PECI (<1s on MCTP)
... Serial PECI won’t be able to make use of OOBMSM HW Primary message router requiring oCode Mailbox handling.

summary: Impact of dropping MCTP on various use cases.
evidence:
Google feedback
... Google has hard time requirement of <1 min polling on OOB health and performance monitoring data (status, temp., % lifetime, bandwidth, etc.). 
... Polling modules on fully populated 2S system takes 22 sec. over PECI (<1s on MCTP)
... Serial PECI prevents monitoring on more than 4S platforms (SPR supports up to 8S).

summary: Impact of PECI implementation on FW update and Nlog collection.
evidence:
Google feedback
... FW update on CPS, performed during “dooms day” or when modules are replaced and one in inventory have mis-matching FW versions, short update window removes validation-intensive requirement of supporting PMEM modules with mis-matched FW versions (we have to avoid it). 
... FW update takes ~10 min / module on PECI vs. 1-2 sec./ module over MCTP
... Fully populated 2S system with 16 DIMMs takes almost 3h (unacceptable)
... Nlog for debug – retrieved by customer for troubleshooting
... takes ~15 minutes/module over PECI instead of ~ 2 sec./DIMM over MCTP

summary: AWS feedback on OOB Telemetry and Crashlog.
evidence:
AWS Feedback
... Must have capabilities related to Intel PMT OOB Telemetry and expanding debuggability using Crashlog. Discovery of telemetry capabilities and crashlog agents presently managed over MCTP.

summary: AWS's backup interface for MCTP drop.
evidence:
AWS Feedback
... Back up interface if we drop MCTP - 
... Discovery is possible using Serial PECI OOB. Limitations include PMT standard doesn’t support streaming over Serial PECI. So streaming telemetry would be dropped.

summary: Ocode team feedback on MCTP feature.
evidence:
Ocode team Feedback –Lukasz
... The feature is mature and is being validated. We don’t have high concern of quality to make it to PRQ. WW 50 - No open bugs on OOBMSM as MCTP bus owner.

summary: Proposal to restructure SIV+MIV Sprint Review for improved collaboration and accountability.
evidence: The current idea is to keep ‘XPIV Retrospective’ as a place to share knowledge and process feedback, and refocus ‘SIV+MIV Sprint Review’ to connect people inside and outside of our validation team.

summary: Proposed approach for the larger group part of SIV+MIV Sprint Review.
evidence:
The idea is to move the review into two parts: first as a larger group and then split off for technical details.

Evidence 2: Build up work groups of people needed for a SPRINT feature/story - technical leadership, customer vision, development, validation, execution

summary: Proposed approach for the technical details part of SIV+MIV Sprint Review.
evidence: After this, we can split off to review technical details as separate work groups (welcome to use Sprint review timeslot if it works for the group)

summary: BMC connection and Peci command execution.
evidence:
The script starts by enabling insecure server connections, then sets up BMC user credentials. It then executes PECI commands to send raw data to the crashdump service on the BMC IP address 10.23.20.143.

 The logs show multiple attempts to execute Peci commands with different parameters.

summary: Error/Trigger: PECI command response.
evidence:
The script executes Peci commands and sends raw data to the crashdump service. The logs show responses from the BMC with PECI command responses, including timestamps and PECIResponse arrays.

  - Response 1:
    PECIResponse: [144, 0, 0, 0, 0]
  - Response 2:
    PECIResponse: [64, 243, 6, 8, 0]

summary: Solution/Scars: Peci command execution.
evidence:
The script executes Peci commands to send raw data to the crashdump service. The logs show multiple attempts to execute Peci commands with different parameters.

  - Command 1:
    PECICommands: [[48, 5, 5, 165, 58, 0, 2, 102, 18, 192]]
  - Command 2:
    PECICommands: [[48, 5, 5, 161, 59, 0]]

summary: Sending Peci commands to Crashdump service for system crash dumps.
evidence: The log shows multiple instances of sending raw PECI commands to the Crashdump service. The commands are related to system crashes and include specific parameters like PPL (Process Load Level) and PECICommands.

summary: Multiple requests to send raw Peci commands.
evidence:
The log shows multiple requests using the following curl command structure: 
`curl -k -u "[REDACTED]:[REDACTED]" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -X POST -d  "{'PECICommands': [[49, 5, 5, 161, 0, 58, 0, 0]]}" https://10.23.20.143/redfish/v1/Systems/system/LogServices/Crashdump/Actions/Oem/Crashdump.SendRawPeci`
The commands are repeated multiple times.
